Tips for Laying Out an Xbox Game Disc

In general, files located on the outer edge of the game disc are accessed faster access than files located in the middle of the disc. Although a file's location on the game disc is the primary factor responsible for access speed, there are many other considerations to keep in mind when arranging a disc's layout.

  • Access Order
  • Accessing Directories
  • The File Cache
  • Security Placeholders
  • Guaranteed Contiguous Space
  • Layer Seek and File Seek
  • Storage and the XDK

Access Order

The Xbox console always reads data from the top of the layout to the bottom. If your title needs to access files sequentially, it is often fastest to arrange those files contiguously from top to bottom in the layout.

For example, if your title must access three media files (named a.x, b.x, and c.x) in order, and you create a disc layout with the files in the opposite order (c.x, b.x, a.x), accessing those files requires the system software to seek to a.x, read a.x, seek to b.x, read b.x, seek to c.x, and read c.x. However, if you place the same data on the disc layout in the order that the title needs to access it (a.x, b.x, c.x), the system software simply seeks to a.x, reads a.x, reads b.x, and reads c.x. Correctly ordering these three files prevents the system from having to make two unnecessary and potentially slow seeks. The optimization wizard can help you arrange files for optimum access speed.

Accessing Directories

Directories are listed in the layout tool along with files, and directories may be moved around within the disc layout in the same way you would move files. The same rules that apply to access time and access order for files also apply to directories.

When accessing a file, the system software must first visit the directory on the disc that contains that file. For example, to access a file named a.x in a directory called media, the system would seek to media, read media, seek to a.x, and read a.x.

The same optimization rules that apply to files also apply to directories. It makes sense to place files in the layout tool as close as possible to the directory that contains them. And, as much as possible, you should place directories and files on the disc in the order in which they are accessed. The optimization wizard takes this approach.

Note    XbGameDisc does not copy empty directories in the layout into the final build of your title.

The File Cache

If two files (named a.x and b.x) are stored in a directory named media, and they are accessed in the order listed above, the system would (conceptually) seek to media, read media, read a.x, seek to media, read media, seek to b.x, and read b.x.

Thanks to the system's file cache, media can be available without seeking the second time. In this case, the access might look more like this: seek to media, read media, read a.x, and read b.x. Of course, the file cache is finite, and there is no guarantee that the parent directory of a file will be immediately available from the cache, but it is important to keep the cache in mind when designing your disc layout.

Security Placeholders

There are some slots on each layer that are reserved for security purposes. You cannot explicitly move the security placeholders. The XbGameDisc tool will automatically move the placeholders to accommodate your game data. After you submit your title, the Xbox team uses these placeholders to integrate security measures to protect the disc.

Guaranteed Contiguous Space

While many hard disks report storage capacities based on a kilobyte of 1000 bytes, the Xbox game disc storage capacities are specified based on a kilobyte of 1024 bytes. This is an important point to keep in mind when planning the layout of your disc.

The total capacity of an Xbox game disc is approximately 9 GB. Not all of that space is available to your title. Some space is reserved for the security placeholders, some is reserved for the video that is played if the game disc is placed in a standard DVD player, and some of the space (near the edges of the disc) is unreliable. The space remaining for use by Xbox titles is approximately 6.4 GB (3.2 GB per layer), but not all of that space is contiguous.

The Xbox game disc is divided in such a way as to guarantee one block of at least 416 MB per layer, with the remaining space on the layer divided into multiple blocks of at least 256 MB each.

Layer Seek and File Seek

Accessing files across layer boundaries is very slow, especially when compared to seeking a file within a given layer. Try to organize your data so that you access it sequentially. Avoid repeatedly crossing the layer boundary.

For instance, a poor disc layout would be one that groups all directories on Layer 0 and all files on Layer 1. In this case, the layer boundary is crossed every time a file is accessed, except in instances where the file cache already contains a reference to a file. Remember, every time a file is accessed, its parent directory is also accessed.

Storage and the XDK

For more information on the Xbox disc drive, see Xbox Storage Devices. You must understand how the Xbox disc drive works to design the layout of your disc in the most efficient way.
